title: 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple Review: Ralph Fiennes and Jack O'Connell Go Full Metal Freakout
text: David Rooney, Chief Film Critic at Hollywood Reporter, reviews the latest installment in the 28 Years Later franchise. Director Nia DaCosta and screenwriter Alex Garland's film shifts from zombie-adjacent horror toward folk horror territory. Ralph Fiennes delivers the most compelling element as Dr. Ian Kelson, a survivalist medic who has constructed a monument to death from human skeletons. Fiennes is described as sinewy and feral in appearance but erudite in manner, bringing both scientific rationality and mystical elements to the role. A subplot involving Kelson's relationship with an infected specimen named Samson introduces surprising tenderness to the series. The doctor attempts communication and treatment through morphine darts. Hildur Gudnadottir's horror score ranges from ecclesiastical passages to unsettling drones. Cinematographer Sean Bobbitt creates dynamic visuals. The reviewer found the Satanist cult storyline more repugnant than scary. The narrative only solidifies when both storylines converge in the third act, delivering amusing and consequential encounters. The film is described as whiplash-inducing but never dull, offering sufficient carnage for horror audiences, with its richest moments belonging to Fiennes' increasingly unhinged visionary.
